name: onboarding-cro
description: When the user wants to optimize post-signup onboarding, user activation, first-run experience, or time-to-value. Also use when the user mentions "onboarding flow," "activation rate," "user activation," "first-run experience," "empty states," "onboarding checklist," "aha moment," or "new user experience." For signup/registration optimization, see signup-flow-cro. For ongoing email sequences, see email-sequence.
capabilities: Audit existing onboarding flows and identify drop-off points
Design activation-focused onboarding checklists and progress indicators
Create effective empty state copy and CTAs
Develop multi-channel onboarding sequences across email and in-app
Build engagement loops that establish user habits
Set up onboarding metrics and funnel analysis frameworks
author: coreyhaines31
securitySummary: All static findings are false positives. The skill is pure markdown documentation about user onboarding optimization. Detected patterns (external_commands, weak_crypto, system_recon) are benign markdown elements like arrows and section headers misidentified by the pattern scanner. No code execution, network calls, or file operations exist.
valueStatement: Slow user activation kills retention and growth. This skill provides expert frameworks for designing onboarding flows that help users reach their aha moment faster, increasing activation rates and long-term retention.
limitations: Does not provide implementation code or technical integration support
Does not access user data or analytics systems
Does not create graphics, UI mockups, or design files
Does not integrate with third-party marketing or analytics tools

useCases.targetUser: Product managers, UX designers, and growth teams
useCases.title: SaaS Product Teams
useCases.description: Product teams designing or optimizing their new user onboarding to improve activation rates and reduce early churn.

useCases.title: Marketing Professionals
useCases.description: Marketing teams creating onboarding email sequences and coordinating in-app messaging with email campaigns.

useCases.title: Startup Founders
useCases.description: Founders validating their onboarding assumptions and identifying quick wins to accelerate time-to-value.

promptTemplates.title: Basic Onboarding Audit
promptTemplates.prompt: Help me audit my product's onboarding flow. My product is a [product type] that helps users [core value proposition]. Current activation rate is [rate]. Where should I focus first?
promptTemplates.scenario: When users want a quick assessment of their onboarding

promptTemplates.title: Onboarding Flow Design
promptTemplates.prompt: Design an onboarding flow for my [B2B/B2C] [product type]. The aha moment is when users [describe activation action]. Include immediate post-signup options, checklist items if needed, and key metrics to track.
promptTemplates.scenario: When building a new onboarding flow from scratch

promptTemplates.title: Drop-off Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: Our onboarding funnel shows [specific drop-off percentages]. Users are dropping at [specific step]. What are the likely causes and specific recommendations to fix this?

promptTemplates.title: Multi-Channel Sequence
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a coordinated email and in-app onboarding sequence for my [product type]. Include trigger timing, email subject lines, and in-app message copy suggestions for days 1 through 7.
promptTemplates.scenario: When building out a full onboarding communication strategy

bestPractices: Focus the entire first session on achieving ONE successful outcome that demonstrates core value
Design empty states as onboarding opportunities with clear CTAs, not dead ends
Coordinate email and in-app messaging to reinforce each other without duplication
antiPatterns: Showing all features at once in a generic product tour that interrupts user goals
Requiring email verification before allowing any product exploration
Blocking access to features until every onboarding step is completed

faq.question: What is the aha moment and how do I identify it?
faq.answer: The aha moment is the action that most strongly correlates with long-term user retention. To find it, analyze what retained users do that churned users do not. Look for the earliest action that predicts future engagement.

faq.question: How many steps should onboarding have?
faq.answer: Keep the critical path to activation as short as possible, ideally 3 steps or fewer. Every additional step creates friction and drop-off. Save advanced features for after activation.

faq.question: Should we use a checklist or a progress bar?
faq.answer: Checklists work best when users have discrete setup tasks. Progress bars work better for completing a single workflow. Use checklists when users need to connect integrations, invite team members, or configure settings.

faq.question: When should we ask for email verification?
faq.answer: Avoid requiring email verification before any exploration. Consider allowing access for 24-48 hours, then require verification to unlock full features. This reduces initial friction while maintaining data quality.

faq.question: How do we handle users who stall during onboarding?
faq.answer: Set up behavioral triggers for stalled users. Send targeted re-engagement emails offering help, simplify the remaining steps, or offer a quick walkthrough option. Monitor recovery rates to measure effectiveness.

faq.question: What metrics should we track for onboarding?
faq.answer: Track activation rate, time to activation, onboarding completion rate, and day 1/7/30 retention. Analyze these by cohort and signup source to identify which user segments need different approaches.
